{"step_by_step_thinking": "Pure left-sided failure typically refers to failure of the left side of the heart, which includes the left atrium and left ventricle. Among the given options, patent ductus arteriosus (c) allows oxygenated blood from the aorta to mix with deoxygenated blood in the pulmonary artery, leading to left-to-right shunting and potentially causing volume overload on the left side of the heart, which can lead to left-sided heart failure. Atrial septal defect (a) also allows shunting from the left to the right side of the heart, but it primarily affects the atria and not the ventricles directly. Aortic stenosis (b) and pulmonary valvular obstruction (d) affect the right side of the heart and do not typically cause pure left-sided failure.", "answer_choice": "B"}
